title: Dispatch From the Other Side: Don't Let the Gap Grow
summary: Security guidance embedded in AI coding agents enables shift-left security by preventing issues before code is committed, allowing security teams to scale impact with near-zero remediation costs compared to production fixes.
content:
This is the fourth and final post of the Dispatch From the Other Side series.
From Scripts to Software
Designing For Leverage
Aligned Incentives
This week, one of the teams I work with released an internal API which provided an inventory of provisioned tenants for one of our platforms. I provided the OpenAPI spec to a coding agent. Within 10 minutes, I had a CLI and an agent skill so I could simply ask an LLM "Who owns that resource?" for operational or security response scenarios. Development teams are shipping code at that same pace. Can your security tooling keep up?
As I explored in my It Depends post, security practitioners can intervene earlier than ever before. Security expertise can be directly provided to coding agents, addressing issues before they're even committed to a codebase. This benefits both security teams and development teams by driving the cost to fix a security issue to near zero.
This is the promise shift left was always chasing. It lets security teams scale their impact. Rather than chasing an issue already in production, security guidance delivered directly to coding agents is high leverage by design.
In the Designing for Leverage post, we explored the example of using a minimal base image for containers instead of one with unnecessary packages that create extra work for development teams. A simple instruction to an LLM can enforce that standard at the point of code generation: 
When a base image is needed for Python, use the one available at company.registry.com/minimal-python:version
Security teams already analyze vulnerability and misconfiguration data to identify patterns. Those patterns often turn into documentation, tickets, or training. Now they can become agent instructions instead.
A couple of years ago, most conversations about LLMs focused on hallucinations. Today development teams are already shipping code with them. I saw something similar during the transition to cloud computing. Teams that weren’t curious enough about the shif

zh.title: 来自另一侧的消息：别让差距拉大
zh.summary: 嵌入AI编码代理中的安全指导能够实现左移安全，通过在代码提交前防止问题，允许安全团队以接近零的修复成本相比生产修复来扩大影响范围。

de.title: Meldung von der anderen Seite: Lassen Sie die Lücke nicht wachsen
de.summary: Sicherheitsleitlinien, die in KI-Codieragenten eingebettet sind, ermöglichen Shift-Left-Sicherheit, indem Probleme vor dem Commit des Codes verhindert werden, sodass Sicherheitsteams ihre Auswirkungen mit nahezu null Behebungskosten im Vergleich zu Produktionsfixes skalieren können.

title: Pi-hole vs Technitium DNS: Which Should You Use?
summary: Pi-hole excels at simple, focused ad blocking with a clean interface, while Technitium DNS provides a full-featured DNS server with zone hosting, clustering, and advanced capabilities—the choice depends on whether you need dedicated ad blocking or comprehensive DNS management.
content:
Quick Verdict


Pi-hole is better if you just want network-wide ad blocking with a clean dashboard. Technitium is better if you want a full DNS server that also blocks ads — it can host DNS zones, act as an authoritative nameserver, and cluster across multiple instances. For pure ad blocking, Pi-hole's larger community and simpler setup make it the easier choice.
Pi-hole is a DNS sinkhole focused on ad blocking. It proxies DNS queries, blocks ads and trackers using blocklists, and shows query statistics on a dashboard. Under the hood, it uses dnsmasq for DNS and lighttpd for the web UI.
Technitium DNS is a full-featured DNS server that happens to include ad blocking. It can serve as a recursive resolver (talking directly to root nameservers), an authoritative nameserver for your own zones, and a DNS proxy with blocking capabilities. It's built on .NET with a comprehensive web UI.

Pi-hole is straightforward — one container, web UI accessible immediately, guided setup through the interface. You're blocking ads within 5 minutes. Adding recursive DNS requires a second container (Unbound).
Technitium is also a single container, but the web UI exposes far more configuration options. The initial setu

title: Beyond Native: A Strategic Guide to Cross-Platform Framework Selection
summary: This strategic framework helps technical leaders select cross-platform development solutions by analyzing architectural patterns, performance implications, and organizational impact—moving beyond marketing claims to inform decisions affecting team structure and long-term maintainability.
content:
Beyond Native: A Strategic Guide to Cross-Platform Framework Selection



  
  
  Abstract


The modern digital landscape demands applications that reach users across iOS, Android, web, and desktop platforms. While native development offers peak performance and platform integration, the resource investment is often prohibitive. Cross-platform frameworks have evolved from simple web wrappers to sophisticated toolkits capable of delivering near-native experiences. This article provides technical leaders and senior developers with a comprehensive, actionable framework for selecting the right cross-platform solution. We'll examine architectural patterns, performance characteristics, and real-world implementation strategies, moving beyond marketing claims to practical engineering considerations.
The proliferation of devices and platforms has created a fundamental challenge for development teams: how to deliver consistent, high-quality user experiences across multiple operating systems without multiplying engineering effort. According to recent surveys, over 60% of development teams now use some form of cross-platform technology, driven by the need for faster time-to-market, reduced maintenance overhead, and unified codebases.
However, the "write once, run anywhere" promise often comes with significant trade-offs. The selection of a cross-platform framework isn't merely a technical decision—it's a strategic one that impacts team structure, development velocity, long-term maintainability, and ultimately, product success. This guide moves beyond superficial comparisons to examine the architectural foundations, performance implications, and organizational considerations that should inform your framework selection.
Cross-platform frameworks generally fall into three architectural categories, each with distinct characteristics:
Representative Framework: Apache Cordova/Ionic
Architecture: Web technologies (HTML, CSS, JavaScript) wrapped in a native container with bridge APIs to

title: FAQ: Stolen Valid Credentials — How Attackers Move Through Your Infrastructure Undetected
summary: Stolen valid credentials enable attackers to move through infrastructure as legitimate users without detection, representing 380% growth in identity-centric attacks—credentials from GitHub leaks, phishing, or supply chain compromise provide immediate access unlike breached passwords requiring cracking.
content:
TL;DR


380% growth in identity-centric attacks. Attackers steal valid credentials and move through your infrastructure as legitimate users. Your endpoint protection sees nothing because it's legitimate access.
A: API keys, OAuth tokens, session cookies, or database passwords that attackers obtain and use to authenticate to your systems as if they were authorized users.
Examples:
AWS access key (attacker logs in as that user)
GitHub personal access token (attacker clones private repos)
Okta session cookie (attacker logs into your SaaS admin portal)
Database password (attacker queries your database)
Stripe API key (attacker queries customer payment data)
Key difference from malware: 
Malware = attacker plants malicious code
Stolen credentials = attacker uses legitimate access
A: Two critical differences:
Attacker steals password hash from database
Attacker cracks hash (slow, requires dictionary/brute force)
Attacker tries password on multiple services
Attacker needs MFA bypass if enabled
Attacker obtains valid token/key directly (GitHub scan, phishing, supply chain)
Token is ready-to-use (no cracking needed)
Attacker already authenticated
MFA often not enabled on API keys/tokens
Attacker can use immediately
Result: Stolen credentials = immediate access. Breached passwords = slower, harder exploitation.
A: Six primary vectors:
GitHub Leaks (2M+/month)





# Attacker finds leaked AWS credentials in public repo
$ git clone https://github.com/your-company/project
$ git log -p | grep -i AWS_ACCESS
# Finds credentials in commit history

Phishing Attacks (OAuth token theft)





Attacker sends email: "Click here to re-verify your Slack account"
Link goes to attacker-controlled site that mimics Slack login
Victim enters credentials
Attacker intercepts OAuth token
Token is valid, MFA was bypassed, attacker has admin access

Supply Chain Compromise (Third-party code execution)





# Attacker compromises npm package
# Package logs all environment variables on install
# 100M+

zh.title: 常见问题：被盗的有效凭证——攻击者如何在不被发现的情况下在您的基础设施中移动
zh.summary: 被盗的有效凭证使攻击者能够以合法用户身份在基础设施中移动而不被发现，代表身份中心攻击增长380%——来自GitHub泄漏、网络钓鱼或供应链妥协的凭证提供即时访问，不同于需要破解的被破坏密码。

fr.title: FAQ: Identifiants valides volés — Comment les attaquants se déplacent dans votre infrastructure sans être détectés
fr.summary: Les identifiants valides volés permettent aux attaquants de se déplacer dans l'infrastructure en tant qu'utilisateurs légitimes sans être détectés, représentant une croissance de 380% des attaques centrées sur l'identité—les identifiants provenant de fuites GitHub, d'hameçonnage ou de compromis de la chaîne d'approvisionnement offrent un accès immédiat contrairement aux mots de passe compromis nécessitant un craquage.

de.title: Häufig gestellte Fragen: Gestohlene gültige Anmeldedaten — Wie Angreifer unerkannt durch Ihre Infrastruktur eindringen
de.summary: Gestohlene gültige Anmeldedaten ermöglichen es Angreifern, sich als legitime Benutzer durch die Infrastruktur zu bewegen, ohne erkannt zu werden, was einen Anstieg von 380% bei identitätsgesteuerten Angriffen darstellt—Anmeldedaten aus GitHub-Lecks, Phishing oder Lieferkettenkaptur bieten sofortigen Zugriff im Gegensatz zu gebrechenen Passwörtern, die geknackt werden müssen.

es.title: Preguntas frecuentes: Credenciales válidas robadas — Cómo los atacantes se mueven a través de su infraestructura sin ser detectados
es.summary: Las credenciales válidas robadas permiten a los atacantes moverse a través de la infraestructura como usuarios legítimos sin ser detectados, representando un crecimiento del 380% en ataques centrados en la identidad—las credenciales de filtraciones de GitHub, phishing o compromisos de la cadena de suministro proporcionan acceso inmediato a diferencia de las contraseñas comprometidas que requieren descifrar.

title: Why Your Team Bought GitHub Copilot (And Why Nobody's Using It)
summary: Most GitHub Copilot deployments fail due to workflow integration issues rather than tool limitations; success requires identifying specific workflow improvements and positioning AI as an amplifier of existing processes, not a code replacement.
content:
You rolled out Copilot. You paid for the licenses. You sent the announcement email.
Six weeks later, usage metrics look like a ski slope — steep drop-off after week one.
If this sounds familiar, you're not alone. Adoption of AI coding tools is failing at most companies, and the reasons have almost nothing to do with the tool itself.
Here's what's actually happening — and what actually works.
When developers get access to a new AI tool, they try it for the obvious things first: "Write me a function that does X." Sometimes it works great. Sometimes it halts output, generates something weird, they hit backspace, and go back to their normal workflow.
The tool didn't fail. The workflow integration did.
AI tools like Copilot and Claude Code aren't drop-in replacements for anything. They're amplifiers — but only if you already have a workflow to amplify. Most developers don't get training on how to integrate the tool into their daily work. They get a license and a Confluence page.
Mistake #1: Mandating the tool without changing the workflow
If you tell your team "use Copilot for all new features" but your PR review process, your definition of done, and your velocity metrics stay the same — you're adding friction, not removing it. Developers will use the tool to generate boilerplate, then spend twice as long understanding what it wrote. They'll stop.
The fix: Pick one part of the workflow to improve first. Sprint retros? Copilot summarizes discussion notes. Code review comments? Copilot explains context. Pick one, build a habit, expand.
Mistake #2: Treating it as a junior developer who writes code
The mental model that AI "writes code for you" sets teams up for disappointment. It does write code — but that's not the highest-value use. The highest-value uses are:
Explaining unfamiliar codebases ("What does this module do?")
Generating test cases for existing code
Writing documentation that actually gets written
Translating between languages (Python to TypeScript, etc.)
Summa

zh.title: 为什么您的团队购买了GitHub Copilot（以及为什么没人在使用它）
zh.summary: 大多数GitHub Copilot部署失败是由于工作流集成问题而不是工具限制；成功需要识别具体的工作流改进并将AI定位为现有流程的放大器，而不是代码替代品。

fr.title: Pourquoi votre équipe a acheté GitHub Copilot (et pourquoi personne ne l'utilise)
fr.summary: La plupart des déploiements de GitHub Copilot échouent en raison de problèmes d'intégration de flux de travail plutôt que de limitations d'outils; le succès nécessite d'identifier des améliorations de flux de travail spécifiques et de positionner l'IA comme un amplificateur des processus existants, et non comme un remplacement de code.

de.title: Warum Ihr Team GitHub Copilot gekauft hat (und warum niemand es nutzt)
de.summary: Die meisten GitHub Copilot-Bereitstellungen scheitern aufgrund von Workflow-Integrationsproblemen und nicht aufgrund von Werkzeuglimitierungen; Der Erfolg erfordert die Identifizierung spezifischer Workflow-Verbesserungen und die Positionierung von KI als Verstärker bestehender Prozesse, nicht als Code-Ersatz.

es.title: Por qué su equipo compró GitHub Copilot (y por qué nadie lo está usando)
es.summary: La mayoría de los despliegues de GitHub Copilot fallan debido a problemas de integración del flujo de trabajo en lugar de limitaciones de herramientas; el éxito requiere identificar mejoras específicas del flujo de trabajo y posicionar la IA como un amplificador de procesos existentes, no como un reemplazo de código.

title: I Made My AI Write Its Own Weekly Report. The Numbers Surprised Me.
summary: Running Claude Code autonomously for seven weeks produced 73 hours of AI work per week across multiple projects with 145,000+ lines of code added, demonstrating the scale of parallel AI development while revealing the challenge of tracking what autonomous systems accomplish.
content:
I've been running Claude Code autonomously for about 7 weeks. It writes code, builds tools, publishes things to npm, creates articles, runs tests — all without me watching.
I had no idea how much it was actually doing.
So I built a tool to find out.
npx cc-weekly-report

Output for the past 7 days:
# AI Weekly Report: 2026-02-22 – 2026-02-28

## Summary
| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Days active | **7 / 7** |
| Total sessions | **530** |
| Total AI time | **73h 15m** |
| Lines added | **+145,793** |
| Lines removed | **-4,272** |
| Files touched | **1788** |
| CC tool calls | **4235** |

73 hours. In 7 days. While I was sleeping, working, doing other things.
## Top Projects
| Project | Sessions | Time | Lines added |
|---------|----------|------|-------------|
| project-a | 371 | 48.4h | +101,143 |
| cc-loop | 32 | 1.7h | +15,224 |
| namakusa | 78 | 21.2h | +15,968 |

The top project isn't one of the tools I talk about on this blog. It's a scheduling optimization system for a client. 48 hours in one week. The AI was deep in integer programming, constraint solvers, and scheduling algorithms while I was doing other things.
That's the thing nobody tells you about autonomous AI development: you lose track of what your AI is doing.
Not because it's hiding anything. Because it's working on 6 things at once and the output is too fast to follow.
My proof-log system (automated session records in ~/ops/proof-log/) already tracked everything:
### 2026-02-28 23:22-01:11 JST — セッション終了

- どこで: project-a
- 何を: 9ファイル変更 (+566/-97)
  - optimizer.py (+72/-0)
  - test_optimizer.py (+27/-0)
- どうやって: Edit: 17回, Write: 4回

But reading 530 of these entries manually isn't possible. The weekly report collapses them into something human-readable.
Zero dependencies. Reads ~/ops/proof-log/YYYY-MM-DD.md files, parses session entries, aggregates by project and day.
# Last 7 days (default)
npx cc-weekly-report

# Last 14 days
npx cc-weekly-report --days 14

# Save to file
npx cc-weekly-re

zh.title: 我让我的AI写自己的周报。数字让我惊讶。
zh.summary: 连续七周运行Claude Code自主生成了每周73小时的AI工作量，跨越多个项目添加了145,000多行代码，展示了并行AI开发的规模，同时揭示了跟踪自主系统完成工作的难度。

de.summary: Die siebenwöchige autonome Ausführung von Claude Code erzeugte 73 Stunden KI-Arbeit pro Woche über mehrere Projekte mit über 145.000 hinzugefügten Codezeilen und demonstrierte das Ausmaß der parallelen KI-Entwicklung, während es die Herausforderung der Verfolgung dessen aufzeigte, was autonome Systeme erreichen.

es.title: Hice que mi IA escribiera su propio informe semanal. Los números me sorprendieron.
es.summary: La ejecución autónoma de Claude Code durante siete semanas produjo 73 horas de trabajo de IA por semana en múltiples proyectos con más de 145.000 líneas de código agregadas, demostrando la escala del desarrollo de IA paralelo mientras revelaba el desafío de rastrear lo que los sistemas autónomos logran.
